does: This system card documents OpenAI's safety evaluation and red teaming processes for the o1 and o1-mini language models before deployment. The work involves systematic frontier risk assessments using OpenAI's Preparedness Framework, covering threat vectors like misuse potential, model limitations, and capability-driven risks. It presents findings from external red teaming efforts where security researchers probed the models for vulnerabilities and harmful behaviors, alongside internal evaluations of the models' reasoning capabilities and failure modes. The card establishes baseline safety metrics and identifies mitigations implemented during development and deployment, providing transparency into how frontier AI systems with extended reasoning were evaluated against potential harms.
connects: This work builds on OpenAI's established safety evaluation practices developed across earlier model releases, extending methodologies proven with GPT-4 and subsequent variants. It responds to the field's growing recognition that frontier models require structured risk frameworks as capabilities advance—connecting to parallel safety work at other labs like Anthropic's Constitutional AI and safety evaluations becoming standard practice. The Preparedness Framework itself represents an evolution of AI risk taxonomy that emerged from policy discussions and safety research literature on frontier model risks. External red teaming reflects maturation of security evaluation practices from applied ML more broadly, while the focus on reasoning-capable systems addresses new safety concerns that emerge when models can engage in extended thought processes before responding.
enables: This work establishes a replicable template for safety evaluation of advanced reasoning systems that the field can build upon. By documenting evaluation approaches and findings transparently, it enables other researchers and organizations to design comparable assessments for their own systems and measure progress on frontier safety challenges. The risk taxonomy and mitigation strategies identified provide concrete anchors for future work on aligning reasoning-oriented models. Publication of the framework facilitates collaboration between academia, industry, and policymakers on standardizing safety practices, potentially enabling regulatory frameworks that rely on structured evaluation. The work also highlights open safety questions around reasoning models—such as emergent deception or optimization problems in extended reasoning—that point toward high-impact research directions for the alignment community.
